title: Dog Thick Yeasty Ear Discharge: Causes, Urgency, and Care
meta_desc: Learn about thick yeasty discharge in dog ears, its urgency level, and why providing photos to your vet helps with faster diagnosis and triage.
content_html:
What is thick yeasty discharge?

A thick, brownish, or tan discharge that often carries a distinct 'corn chip' or musty odor is a hallmark sign of a yeast overgrowth, specifically Malassezia. This occurs when the natural environment of the ear canal is disrupted—often by moisture, allergies, or trapped debris—allowing yeast to multiply uncontrollably. It causes the lining of the ear to become inflamed, itchy, and uncomfortable for your pet.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for a yeasty ear infection is Medium. While it is generally not a life-threatening situation, it is a significant welfare concern because it is very painful and itchy. You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. Delaying treatment can lead to more severe issues, such as a secondary bacterial infection, a ruptured eardrum, or an aural hematoma caused by excessive head shaking.

Why a photo helps with triage

When you provide a clear, well-lit photo of your dog's ear canal and the discharge, it allows a veterinary professional to assess the severity of the inflammation and the amount of buildup. Visual evidence helps the triage team determine if your dog needs to be squeezed in for an urgent appointment or if it can wait a day. It also helps the vet prepare for specific diagnostic tests, like an ear cytology, before you even arrive.

faqs.question: Can I clean my dog's ears at home before the vet visit?
faqs.answer: It is usually best to wait until after the vet has examined the ear. Cleaning the ear can remove the material the vet needs to sample for a diagnosis and may push debris deeper if the eardrum is ruptured.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of an ear infection besides discharge?
faqs.answer: Common signs include frequent head shaking, scratching at the ear, redness or swelling of the ear flap, and a sensitivity to being touched around the head.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to yeasty ears?
faqs.answer: Yes, breeds with floppy ears (like Cocker Spaniels or Basset Hounds) or those with hairy ear canals (like Poodles) are more prone to yeast infections because their ear structure traps moisture.
